part: A House Full of Music in Richmond
title: The First Black Band Director in Richmond
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier on the Rutgers campus in Newark on July 20, 2011, Junius Williams opens on his Virginia roots: born in Suffolk, he moved to Richmond at five, the son of two music teachers, his father the first black band director in Richmond and his mother the first coordinator of music for North Virginia schools, in a house full of music with his brother John.

part: A House Full of Music in Richmond
title: Armstrong or Walker
summary: Williams describes starting school early at his mother's urging, the insulated middle-class world his parents negotiated, and his father steering him from Maggie Walker, where his friends were headed, to Armstrong High School, one of Richmond's two black high schools.

part: A House Full of Music in Richmond
title: A Classic, Not a Game
summary: He recalls the Maggie Walker-Armstrong football classic that filled city stadium with thirty thousand people and ordered black social life, playing tennis with his Richmond contemporary Arthur Ashe, and the Baptist church music that surrounded his family at Ebenezer Baptist Church.

part: A House Full of Music in Richmond
title: We're Not Going to March in the Back
summary: Williams remembers the picture of Emmett Till's body in Jet magazine igniting anger in him as a boy near Till's age, and a family civil rights victory when his father and Walker's Joe Kennedy refused to put their black bands at the back of Richmond's segregated Tobacco Festival Parade, the black drummers outplaying the white schools when the parade was integrated.

title: He Wouldn't Let Me Play the Pickup Bands
summary: Williams explains that his father, a professional musician who knew the precarious life of bands, forbade him the Richmond pickup bands and discouraged a music career, how he applied to Northwestern for its marching band but took Amherst College's full scholarship instead, and how, with Amherst's music program so poor he set his saxophone down, he later took up the harmonica from a movement visitor.

title: The Fire Was Still Burning
summary: He recounts Charles Sherrod leading Virginia Union students in sit-ins at downtown Richmond department stores around 1960 and 1961, his parents using the claim that college students barred high schoolers to keep him out of harm's way, and resolving that he had to go south once he reached Amherst in 1961.

title: The Color of His Neck Was Red
summary: Williams describes the household conversations from Emmett Till to the Montgomery bus boycott and Brown, and the day Richmond's buses were desegregated, when he and his brother John, warned by their parents to be careful, sat just behind the red-necked driver and drew quiet smiles from the older black riders who had wanted to do it for years.

title: BB Guns Across the Ravine
summary: He tells of white boys shooting BB guns across a wooded ravine by his family's Barton Avenue house and the bamboo weapons the black boys made in return, getting shot in the hand trying to make friends, the off-limits white blocks like Brooklyn Park Boulevard, and a Richmond so insulated he had no white friends his own age.

title: Why Don't You Be a Lawyer
summary: Williams recounts the idea of law stealing into his consciousness at Amherst while he meant to become a SNCC field secretary, his parents pushing law because they saw his civil rights bent, and his early activism: voter registration on spring break and in Springfield, Massachusetts, and a conference he organized in the winter of 1965.

title: If Junius White Says He's Okay
summary: He describes the Richmond civil rights lawyer Oliver Hill as a family friend and neighbor who attended Howard with his mother, tells how Hill once called Williams's grandfather Junius White to vouch for his release after a Suffolk arrest on the way to argue a teacher equal-pay case, and his own high-stakes gamble to apply only to Columbia and Yale, getting in.

title: Pop Williams on the Front Porch
summary: Williams names his father, known to the neighborhood kids as Pop Williams for speaking to everyone from the front porch, as his closest role model, and describes the best-and-worst-of-times shock of arriving at all-male Amherst in fall 1961, where summers among white people had spared him culture shock but there was now no enclave to retreat to.

title: There Was a Quota
summary: He recalls the four black students per class at Amherst and the matching quotas at Smith and Mount Holyoke, the dean who recruited him, the strained dance mixers where he was told no more than yes, and being unwilling to attempt interracial dating in that era, while still finding good roommates at Pratt Dormitory.

title: The First at Alpha Delta Phi
summary: Williams describes rushing fraternities as a freshman because fraternity houses were the only social life, choosing the exclusive Alpha Delta Phi, which had never had a black member, over Theta Delta Chi, which he felt wanted him only for being black, and largely being welcomed there as the first.

title: I Couldn't Catch on to Calculus
summary: He recounts how unprepared Southern schooling left him in calculus and physics, dragging him to a C average that bruised an ego accustomed to being valedictorian, and the math professor Bailey Brown who restored an answer he had erased and gave him an A he then celebrated across campus, only for the C to follow him after a failed final.

title: You Got to Do the Class Analysis
summary: Williams describes finding his footing in political science and economics, and how working in Harlem in 1964 alongside Julian Houston and the daughter of Norman Cousins, who understood Marxist analysis he had been taught to reject, sent him to Professor Colston Warne's course, where he first read Marx and gained the lens of class beyond race.

title: President of Students for Racial Equality
topic: An Editorial and a Campus Group
summary: He tells how an anonymous Amherst Student article attacking civil rights drew his rebuttal editorial, praised by a once-harsh English teacher as understated emotion, which made his name synonymous with the cause and led to his leading Students for Racial Equality, raising money for jailed activists and meeting Cordell Reagon when the Freedom Singers sang at Smith.

title: The Fat Man With the Camera
summary: Williams recounts his first demonstration on a 1963 Richmond picket line with the NAACP youth Commandos, photographed by a government man sent to intimidate them, his voter registration work, meeting Marian Wright Edelman at Amherst, and her steering him to Operation Crossroads Africa, which sent him on scholarship to spend ten weeks in Uganda's Kigezi District.

title: Off the Plane Into the March
summary: He describes landing in New York from Uganda on the day of the March on Washington, August 1963, going with his brother John, and being most moved by the SNCC singers performing the songs from Danville, having seen a foreign newspaper photo of that summer's violence and regretted being away.

title: Reform or Revolution
summary: Williams describes convening a large February 1965 conference titled Civil Rights Movement: Reform or Revolution, drawn to the thrilling, much-vilified Malcolm X he had not yet met, and choosing Ossie Davis for his civil rights record and Michael Harrington as the man said to have discovered poverty for the opening session.

title: Talking to Malcolm X on a Wall Phone
summary: He recounts getting Malcolm X's home number through the network and, on a public wall phone with onlookers, persuading him to come for a thousand-dollar honorarium, then filling out the program with civil rights luminaries and trench organizers from CORE, SNCC, and the Northern Student Movement.

title: Down to Ossie Davis
summary: Williams tells how Malcolm X was stranded abroad over a visa and Michael Harrington stopped by an ice storm, leaving only Ossie Davis, who took the stage at Mount Holyoke before a thousand people, voiced the absent speakers' roles plus his own, won a standing ovation, and gave the conference legitimacy, with Williams concluding he was becoming a revolutionary.

title: Would They Have Killed Him Here
summary: Williams recalls Malcolm X being killed at the Audubon Ballroom the week after the conference, wondering whether someone might have tried it in the lonely hills around South Hadley, and his reaction of anger, sadness, and regret at having had only one phone conversation with a man whose voice and honesty he had liked.

title: It Was Time to Go South
summary: He describes how his small group, after the conference, decided in March 1965 to join the Selma to Montgomery march, feeling they could not be revolutionaries without front-line experience, and piling about seven students from UMass, Mount Holyoke, Smith, and Amherst into a Volkswagen bus.

title: Take My Station Wagon
summary: Williams recounts stopping at his parents' Richmond house, where his father swapped the troubled Volkswagen for his big Buick station wagon, the two black riders hiding under clothes when a South Carolina cop stopped them and quick-thinking Fred Arano claimed they were bound for a Florida spring fling, and a semi-trailer collision in Georgia that a friendly gas station fixed.

title: We Need You Here in Montgomery
summary: He describes reaching the Montgomery SNCC office at Jackson and High Street, where Stokely Carmichael redirected them into SNCC's parallel operation born of distrust of King and SCLC, and what Williams later pieced together: that Jimmie Lee Jackson's killing and John Lewis's beaten march predated SCLC's involvement, which came only after Johnson pressed a judge to lift the injunction.

title: This May Be the Last Time
summary: Williams recounts SNCC commanding Montgomery so the police had to bottle the demonstrators at Jackson and High Street, where mounted men with long cattle sticks, rumored deputized Klansmen, beat them back each time they marched, until one charge halted as the trapped crowd sang at a church that never opened its door, the horses turning away in a moment that revised his thinking about God.

title: He Stopped Us on One Leg
summary: He describes watching Lyndon Johnson say we shall overcome on television while violence raged in the street and judging him a hypocrite, and the Puerto Rican demonstrator who, after a motorcycle cop knelt to taunt then struck him, leapt up on his injured leg to stop the crowd from seizing the scattered officer, sparing them from being branded terrorists.

title: King Marched Us Two Blocks
summary: Williams recounts King coming over as the march drew press, the barricades opening only for King to lead them two blocks and turn back while cops grinned and threw peanuts, and the SNCC organizers then leading them out a back way to empty Alabama State University into the streets, the crowd now mostly black, until a young woman was run down by a horse.

title: Brother, They Got Too Many Guns
topic: Jumping on the Man With the Brick
summary: He tells of jumping on the back of a lineman-sized man who picked up a brick after a horse ran down a young woman he called his sister, talking him out of throwing it over a block until she rose unhurt, an episode Williams turned into a rap his singing group performed, before the marchers sat down downtown and were arrested.

title: Unless Everybody Had a Blanket
summary: Williams describes being jailed in a Kilby Prison bullpen of seventy-five men with too few dusty blankets the biggest men seized, until the small, bespectacled organizer Worth Long stood on a table and declared he would take no blanket unless everyone had one, shaming the men into sharing and, when the jailers claimed there were no more, winning more blankets through collective refusal.

title: My First Leadership Role
summary: He recounts that after Worth Long was put in solitary for trying to integrate the church service, he and the Detroit SNCC member Stu House led the cell for seven days, keeping morale up with freedom songs and refereeing fights over food, and how, money raised in Massachusetts to bail him out forcing his early release, he reluctantly left for the staging ground outside town.

title: I Wanted to Be Just Like Him
topic: Ivanhoe Donaldson and the Rear Jeep
summary: Williams describes joining the rainy staging ground with its stars Harry Belafonte and Nina Simone as one of the celebrated Kilby Prison releasees, admiring the calm, focused logistics man Ivanhoe Donaldson in his yellow rain slicker, and riding in the very last first-aid jeep of the march into Montgomery, exposed to a feared shot from behind.

title: He Calmed the Waters
summary: In a reflective aside Williams speaks of Worth Long as an ethnomusicologist who sought out old blues singers and made a film about them, too sick to attend the fiftieth anniversary, recalling him as a man he wanted to be like for calming the waters, and mentions Posey Lombard, a white artist from Smith who went to work in Georgia for the movement.

title: Nobody Told Us, We Just Evolved
summary: Williams reflects on how he and Stu House simply evolved into leading the seventy-five men, crediting his experience running a college civil rights group and Stu's time with SNCC, and recounts hearing on the radio in his father's station wagon, driven by Posey, that Viola Liuzzo had been killed, picking up a screwdriver and vowing his would not be a nonviolent confrontation.

part: Tom Hayden Calls Him to Newark
title: The Civil Rights Movement Is Over
summary: He recounts Tom Hayden seeking him out in the summer of 1965, telling him the civil rights movement was over with the voting rights law in hand and recruiting him to Newark on the strength of his conference and Alabama work, and Williams deciding he could go to Newark and start law school at Yale, making Newark his home if he liked it.

part: Tom Hayden Calls Him to Newark
title: The Movement Had Already Come North
summary: Williams explains that the movement had come north through efforts like his Harlem playground work, that SDS and its ERAP projects came to Newark on the theory that class mattered more than race, and how Hayden's SDS group split from the older white Jewish liberals of the Clinton Hill Neighborhood Corporation and moved down into the poorer, mostly black lower Clinton Hill.

part: Tom Hayden Calls Him to Newark
title: It Was the Working Poor
summary: He describes finding, as Betty Maul explained, that Newark's people had jobs that were merely bad and underpaid, the dawning era of the working poor, while what stirred them was police brutality, gouging landlords, and store credit bait-and-switch, the issues the Newark Community Union Project took on as Williams set out to be an organizer on a frontier SNCC could not match.

title: Let the People Decide
topic: A Coalition of Radicals and Liberals
summary: Williams describes the wider Newark coalition against the forces keeping people down: CORE, the group Anvil, black labor unions, and preachers outside Mayor Hugh Addonizio's reach, including white rabbis dragged through the mud at the Barringer High School jobs protest, with the NCUP students as the radicals insisting the people decide in meetings that ran all night, led in fact by Tom Hayden alongside Terry Jefferson, Bessie and Thurmond Smith.

title: Let CORE Deal With That
summary: He recounts that some NCUP organizers found police brutality too volatile because it was nakedly racial, white cops beating black heads, and left it to CORE's weekly demonstrations, and how his very first day in Newark he joined a CORE protest over a man shot by an officer named Hank Martinez, who years later won a City Council seat and became his friend.

title: Race Was a Bigger Issue Than They Could Deal With
summary: Williams reflects that within weeks he saw race would overwhelm what the white SDS students could handle, that he and Phil were the two black college organizers, and how SNCC's 1966 turn telling whites to organize whites, Carmichael replacing Lewis at a tumultuous meeting, and the coming purge reflected a wider conviction, voiced by Malcolm X and the nationalists, that race was predominant as the integrationists, even King, lost ground.

title: I Can Work With You, But Not Her
topic: Organizing a Block for a Stoplight
summary: He describes a SNCC office opening in Newark's Central Ward beside the NCUP hangout Hayden cultivated, taking the black power line under Phil, and Williams being assigned with Corinna to break open a new Central Ward block over the residents' demand for a stoplight, where a man named Billy who knew the Nation of Islam line would work with him but not with his white partner, testing his commitment to an integrated, class-based movement.

title: Just a Grunt They Still Watched
summary: Williams recounts the evidence he was under surveillance though only a low-level organizer: a 1968 HUAC proceeding where a policeman produced a private letter to Hayden and accused him of living with the SNCC hell-raiser Phil Hutchings, an FBI search of his suitcase after a California trip to see his sister, a Paris automat reunion with a young woman named Jackie he had met in Russia who pressed him about his itinerary and hurried away, and informers' reports he later found in Governor Hughes's files in Trenton.

title: Up Against the Car
summary: Williams recounts returning from a Philadelphia black power conference as the 1967 Newark Rebellion erupted, slipping into the sealed city by the streets he knew, and being pulled over after curfew with three other men by police with drawn pistols and shotguns, saved when a sergeant saw the law books he had left in the car and overruled officers who wanted to shoot them.

title: Then the State Police Came In
summary: He describes laying low with Phil at a basement apartment the HUAC report misaddressed, watching people glad to turn the tide, and how things worsened when the state police and National Guard arrived and the police rioted, so that his team of law student VISTAs investigating urban renewal relocation turned instead to interviewing witnesses to the police violence.

title: Shot Forty-Six Times
summary: Williams recounts a witness account of state police systematically shooting a young looter dozens of times, including in the top of the head, the information he turned over through the Legal Services Project to a grand jury that treated him as the suspect, Amiri Baraka's flyer of the destroyed corpse, and his retort that what was inflammatory was the man being shot forty-six times.

part: United Brothers and the Medical School
title: After the Rebellion, Everybody Wanted to Talk
summary: He describes the homeowner-organizer Louise Epperson, fighting the proposed College of Medicine and Dentistry that would take two hundred acres, finding that after the Newark Rebellion the power structure suddenly listened, and Williams resolving to capture that energy and the image of the black man with the brick to force lasting change.

part: United Brothers and the Medical School
title: Nineteen Acres by Going Up
topic: A Smaller Footprint for the Medical School
summary: Williams recounts Amiri Baraka rising to prominence after being beaten in the rebellion and keynoting the black power conference, while Williams and others, after the community lost an earlier lawsuit over the medical school, formed the Newark Area Planning Association and asked Yale planners for the smallest footprint, who offered nineteen acres by building up against the proposed clearance of well over a hundred sixty.

part: United Brothers and the Medical School
title: One Percent Vacancy
topic: The Lawsuit and the United Brothers
summary: He describes his cold candy-store office, bringing in the NAACP Legal Defense Fund under Jack Greenberg to file a housing-insufficiency complaint to HUD and HEW built on his VISTAs' research showing a one percent vacancy rate, and Amiri Baraka asking him in December 1967 to join the United Brothers, formed to unite the community and elect Newark's first black mayor.

part: United Brothers and the Medical School
title: A Nine-Person Negotiating Team
summary: Williams recounts a coalition of NAPA and Louise Epperson's Committee Against Negro and Puerto Rican Removal forcing the state to the table, holding to a nine-person negotiating team backed by two hundred supporters who shut down would-be speakers, and negotiating with officials including Paul Ylvisaker and Ralph Dungan the medical school agreements that yielded sixty acres for community housing.
keywords: Committee Against Negro and Puerto Rican Removal

part: United Brothers and the Medical School
title: That Invisible Brother With the Brick
summary: He describes winning in the agreements a construction-trades training program for blacks and Latinos, the throwing out of the mayor's Model Cities committee and his own election to its replacement, and a multi-stakeholder body of unions, contractors, and the community to monitor compliance, all backed by the unspoken memory of the man with the brick.

title: May the Best Man Win, and We Hope It's Not Williams
summary: Williams recounts becoming Kenneth Gibson's first campaign manager in 1970, the in-community fight over who would get the sixty acres while white onlookers hoped Williams would lose, the housing council that decided the land and produced a thousand units, and the construction-trades corporation that won hundreds of young men union books with backing from Gus Henningburg and others.

title: Twenty-Six and Making Twenty-Six Thousand
summary: He describes being put in charge of planning most of the federal money coming to Newark at age twenty-six, the friction his salary caused, the street momentum that came from the civil rights and black power groups rather than the Democratic Party, and his distaste, shared by many, for the cultural nationalism of Amiri Baraka's Committee for a Unified Newark that helped elect Gibson.

title: They Said I Stole the Money
summary: Williams recounts Gibson, wanting neither him nor Baraka in leadership, eventually firing him over a year-old audit interpretation dispute revived by Congressman Joseph Minish, who implied on the front page of the Star-Ledger that Williams had stolen three hundred thousand dollars, even as Williams had doubled the city's funds and brought in the fifteen-million-dollar Project Rehab.

title: Bring Me Down What You Have
summary: He describes turning the story around by persuading Star-Ledger editor Mort Pye to run his response on the Wednesday front page, a television host declaring him a good guy to applause, and forcing Congressman Minish into a tie at a hearing, where Williams, a lawyer who knew the material, defended himself though his entire staff stayed away.

title: There Is Nothing There
topic: Fired, and Vindicated by an Auditor
summary: Williams recounts Gibson giving him thirty days then firing him, a rally of four hundred where Raymond Brown asked why the mayor was absent and trying to get rid of Williams, and an auditor approaching him later at an airport to reveal he had told the prosecutor there was nothing to indict him on, while Baraka, similarly thwarted by Gibson, abandoned cultural nationalism to become a Marxist.

part: President of the National Bar Association
title: Plan My Convention
topic: Rising in the National Bar Association
summary: He describes leaving City Hall for law practice and being drawn by O.T. Wells into the National Bar Association, the black analog to the ABA, organizing Wells's 1973 San Francisco convention on his own dime after a Los Angeles fall-through, and building from that the network that became his organizational base.

part: President of the National Bar Association
title: A Coalition of Young and Women Lawyers
summary: Williams recounts reviving the Young Lawyers Division as a vehicle, partnering with the newly formed women lawyers division steeped in movement-era democracy against an imperial old guard, and parlaying that coalition through the vice-presidential offices and delegation trade-offs until he ran unopposed for president-elect.

part: Zimbabwe and the Eyes on the Prize
title: Adopted by the United Nations
summary: Williams describes becoming the youngest president of the National Bar Association at thirty-four in 1978 and introducing the cause of southern African independence, his international affairs committee under Julius Chambers producing a paper exposing the sham of white-controlled constitutions in southern Rhodesia, which a SNCC friend helped carry to the United Nations, where it was adopted as an official document on Zimbabwean independence and translated into seven languages.

part: Zimbabwe and the Eyes on the Prize
title: Hold On to Sanctions
topic: From the OAU to Jimmy Carter
summary: He recounts the paper opening doors among African diplomats and at the OAU, invitations to Jamaica and Cuba, a thwarted plan to bring black professionals to a newly independent Zimbabwe, and the crowning report that his White House contacts got the document to Jimmy Carter to help hold sanctions, followed by a 1979 Los Angeles convention where Louis Farrakhan, whom he had met in New Haven, mesmerized the lawyers, and a semester at Harvard's Institute of Politics.

part: A Mayoral Race and a Return to Organizing
title: Power in the Suite, Not the Street
summary: Williams describes returning from Harvard to find Gibson twelve years in office with little changed, having divorced the power of the people from the institutions, and resolving to build another movement and run for mayor in 1982, a race he lost for lack of money, concluding that being all things to all people to raise funds was not for him, before serving as town attorney in Irvington under Mayor Sara Bost.

part: A Mayoral Race and a Return to Organizing
title: Thorough and Efficient
topic: Abbott v. Burke and Organizing Parents
summary: He recounts the Abbott v. Burke school-funding case, brought by the Education Law Center in 1981 under the state constitution's thorough and efficient clause and upheld so cities must get suburban-level funding no governor has fully granted, and Williams's Rutgers-based effort, funded by foundations, to teach parents and young people how to get organized rather than building one more organization.

part: A Mayoral Race and a Return to Organizing
title: Your Voice Deserves to Be Heard
summary: Williams closes on teaching parents to use information and strategy and young people that their voices, once amplified through media skills they learn over a summer program, deserve to be heard, lamenting that the concept of organization was lost when the movement put its eggs in electing black politicians, and pointing to his coming book, Unfinished Agenda: Urban Politics in the Era of Black Power.
